findings.title: yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag: GitHub Actions step uses a mutable tag or branch reference. Tags and branch names can be silently repointed by the action owner, enabling supply-chain attacks — as seen in the trivy-action and kics-github-action compromises. Pin the reference to a full 40-character commit SHA instead, e.g. `uses: actions/checkout@8ade135a41bc03ea155e62e844d188df1ea18608`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag detected: GitHub Actions step uses a mutable tag or branch reference. Tags and branch names can be silently repointed by the action owner, enabling supply-chain attacks — as seen in the trivy-action and kics-github-action compromises. Pin the reference to a full 40-character commit SHA instead, e.g. `uses: actions/checkout@8ade135a41bc03ea155e62e844d188df1ea18608`.

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can compromise the GitHub Actions workflow or trick a maintainer into merging a malicious change to the workflow file. The attacker must be able to modify the repository's workflow YAML or the referenced action's tag/branch.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker modifies the workflow file (deploy.yml line 14) to point to a malicious action tag or branch. 2. The next time the workflow runs (e.g., on push or schedule), GitHub Actions executes the malicious action. 3. The malicious action can exfiltrate secrets, modify code, or deploy compromised artifacts.
attack_scenario.impact: Supply-chain compromise: attacker gains access to CI/CD secrets (e.g., deployment keys, cloud credentials) and can deploy malicious code to production or steal sensitive data.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding is real because the workflow uses a mutable tag/branch reference, which is a known supply-chain risk. The attack path is concrete: modify the workflow or the referenced action's tag to execute arbitrary code in the CI/CD pipeline.

findings.cwe: CWE-1357: Reliance on Insufficiently Trustworthy Component
findings.tool_output: Rule: yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software and Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
findings.description:
Semgrep rule semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response detected: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The finding is a duplicate of IRON-SEMGREP-003 through IRON-SEMGREP-005 (same file, same line). Additionally, the description says 'requires login' which suggests the endpoint is authenticated, reducing the attack surface. Without seeing the actual code, it's likely a false positive because the input may be used in a safe context (e.g., flash message, redirect, or ORM query).]
[Also reported by another step: Semgrep rule semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response detected: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().

findings.title: python.flask.security.open-redirect.open-redirect: Data from request is passed to redirect(). This is an open redirect and could be exploited. Consider using 'url_for()' to generate links to known locations. If you must use a URL to unknown pages, consider using 'urlparse()' or similar and checking if the 'netloc' property is the same as your site's host name. See the references for more information.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.open-redirect.open-redirect detected: Data from request is passed to redirect(). This is an open redirect and could be exploited. Consider using 'url_for()' to generate links to known locations. If you must use a URL to unknown pages, consider using 'urlparse()' or similar and checking if the 'netloc' property is the same as your site's host name. See the references for more information.
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The finding says 'requires login', meaning the endpoint is authenticated. Open redirects are less impactful when the attacker must be authenticated. Additionally, without seeing the actual code, it's possible the redirect uses a safe pattern (e.g., redirect to a known internal URL or uses url_for). The finding is likely a false positive.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The finding says 'requires login', meaning the endpoint is authenticated. Open redirects are less impactful when the attacker must be authenticated. Additionally, without seeing the actual code, it's possible the redirect uses a safe pattern (e.g., redirect to a known internal URL or uses url_for). The finding is likely a false positive.

findings.title: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.
findings.description: Semgrep rule html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity detected: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.

attack_scenario.explanation: Missing integrity attribute on a CDN resource is a best-practice issue, not an exploitable vulnerability without a compromised CDN. The attacker would need to compromise the CDN, which is a separate, high-complexity precondition. Without evidence of CDN compromise, this is a low-severity finding, not a real vulnerability.

findings.cwe: CWE-353: Missing Support for Integrity Check
findings.tool_output: Rule: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token detected: Manually-created forms in django templates should specify a csrf_token to prevent CSRF attacks.

attack_scenario.explanation: This is a Django-specific rule applied to a Flask project. The template index.html is likely a Flask template, not Django. CSRF protection in Flask is handled differently (e.g., Flask-WTF). The rule is misapplied, making this a false positive.

findings.tool_output: Rule: python.django.security.django-no-csrf-token.django-no-csrf-token | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: <nil>

findings.title: python.flask.security.audit.app-run-param-config.avoid_app_run_with_bad_host: Running flask app with host 0.0.0.0 could expose the server publicly.
findings.description: Semgrep rule python.flask.security.audit.app-run-param-config.avoid_app_run_with_bad_host detected: Running flask app with host 0.0.0.0 could expose the server publicly.

attack_scenario.explanation: Running Flask on 0.0.0.0 is a configuration issue, not a direct vulnerability. It may expose the server to the network, but without debug mode enabled, the risk is limited. The finding does not specify if debug=True is set. Likely a low-severity configuration warning, not a real vulnerability.

findings.cwe: CWE-668: Exposure of Resource to Wrong Sphere
findings.tool_output: Rule: python.flask.security.audit.app-run-param-config.avoid_app_run_with_bad_host | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A01:2021 - Broken Access Control A01:2025 - Broken Access Control]

findings.title: SBOM generated: 17 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 17 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).

findings.title: 1/10 recent commits are not GPG signed
findings.description: Unsigned commits can be forged by attackers who gain access to git configuration. GPG-sign all commits to establish cryptographic proof of authorship.
findings.fix_suggestion: Configure git GPG signing: git config --global commit.gpgsign true

findings.title: Unpinned GitHub Action in deploy.yml
findings.description: GitHub Actions should be pinned to commit SHA, not version tags. Tags can be moved by attackers who compromise the action repository (GhostAction 2025 attack vector).
findings.fix_suggestion: Pin actions to full commit SHA: uses: actions/checkout@a81bb... instead of @v4

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.
